name: Ajofrin, Orgaz y Mora – circular desde Mascaraque
name_original: Ajofrín, Orgaz y Mora – circular desde Mascaraque
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Mascaraque are characterized by accessible, well-paved surfaces suitable for various fitness levels. The surrounding La Mancha region features extensive cycling networks, including routes that traverse wetlands, rivers, lagoons, and rolling hills. Cyclists can expect scenic views of traditional La Mancha landscapes, with routes ranging from short excursions to longer, more challenging rides. Best road cycling routes around Mascaraque The most popular road cycling route is Orgaz, Marjaliza Pass, and Los Yébenes Loop, a 19.9 miles (32.1 km) trail that takes 1 hour 30 minutes to complete. This moderate route includes a pass and connects several towns. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Mora and Villanueva de Bogas Loop, an easy 20.4 miles (32.8 km) path. It offers accessible cycling through the local countryside. Local road cyclists also love the Ajofrin, Orgaz y Mora – circular desde Mascaraque, a 29.0 miles (46.7 km) trail leading through the towns of Ajofrin, Orgaz, and Mora, often completed in about 1 hour 54 minutes. Road cycling around Mascaraque is defined by well-paved surfaces, rolling hills, and traditional La Mancha landscapes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy loops to moderate challenges. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 100 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Mascaraque's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Mascaraque?
A: Road cycling routes in the Mascaraque area feature mostly well-paved surfaces, making them suitable for various fitness levels. You'll encounter rolling hills and traditional La Mancha landscapes, with some routes including moderate elevation gains, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1366247798/orgaz-marjaliza-pass-and-los-yebenes-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Orgaz, Marjaliza Pass, and Los Yébenes Loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Mascaraque?
A: Yes, Mascaraque offers several easy road cycling options. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1366267305/mora-and-villanueva-de-bogas-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mora and Villanueva de Bogas Loop&lt;/a&gt; is an accessible 20.4-mile (32.8 km) path that provides a pleasant ride through the local countryside with minimal elevation.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes starting from Mascaraque?
A: Absolutely. Many routes in the region are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027676549/ajofrin-orgaz-y-mora-circular-desde-mascaraque" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ajofrin, Orgaz y Mora – circular desde Mascaraque&lt;/a&gt;, which is a 29.0-mile (46.7 km) trail connecting several towns.

Q: What are some scenic highlights or landmarks I can see while cycling near Mascaraque?
A: The region offers scenic views of traditional La Mancha landscapes. Along your rides, you can explore historical sites like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2127963" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almonacid Castle&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2210456" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of the Counts of Orgaz&lt;/a&gt;. The broader La Mancha region is also known for its iconic windmills, famously associated with the 'Route of Don Quixote'.

Q: How long do road cycling routes around Mascaraque typically take to complete?
A: Route durations vary depending on distance and your pace. Shorter, easy excursions like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1366267305/mora-and-villanueva-de-bogas-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mora and Villanueva de Bogas Loop&lt;/a&gt; can take around 1 hour 25 minutes, while longer, more challenging rides such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38217060" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Orgaz – Umbría del Madroñal loop from Mora&lt;/a&gt; might require over 3 hours.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Mascaraque?
A: The La Mancha region generally offers pleasant cycling conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn are often ideal, providing comfortable temperatures for longer rides. While summer can be warm, early mornings or late afternoons can still be suitable for cycling.

Q: Are there any challenging road cycling routes with significant climbs near Mascaraque?
A: While many routes feature rolling hills, some offer more substantial climbs. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1366247798/orgaz-marjaliza-pass-and-los-yebenes-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Orgaz, Marjaliza Pass, and Los Yébenes Loop&lt;/a&gt;, for example, includes a pass and an elevation gain of over 400 meters, providing a moderate challenge for road cyclists.

Q: What kind of natural features can I expect to see on road cycling routes in the Mascaraque area?
A: The wider La Mancha region, encompassing Mascaraque, is known for its diverse landscapes. Cyclists can traverse areas featuring wetlands, rivers, lagoons, and rolling hills, offering varied natural scenery beyond the traditional agricultural views.

Q: Are there options for longer road cycling tours around Mascaraque?
A: Yes, for those seeking longer rides, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38217060" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Orgaz – Umbría del Madroñal loop from Mora&lt;/a&gt; extend to nearly 70 km (43.5 miles), offering a more extensive exploration of the region and requiring good fitness.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Mascaraque?
A: Road cyclists frequently appreciate the well-paved surfaces and the accessibility of routes for various fitness levels. The opportunity to experience traditional La Mancha landscapes and connect through charming towns like Orgaz and Mora is also a highlight for many.

Q: Are there any less crowded road cycling routes in the Mascaraque area?
A: The extensive cycling networks throughout La Mancha mean you can often find quieter routes, especially away from the immediate vicinity of larger towns. Exploring some of the lesser-known loops, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24533168" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Orgaz – Mascaraque loop from Mora&lt;/a&gt;, might offer a more tranquil experience.
tips:
The Church of Santa María Magdalena in Ajofrín (Toledo)
Construction: Late 15th and early 16th centuries, built on the site of an earlier 12th-century church.
Style: Transitional between Mudéjar Gothic and Renaissance.
Floor Plan: Single nave in the shape of a Latin cross, with a square transept and deep transepts.
Interior Details:

Ribbed vaulting supported by corbels decorated with figures (birds with human heads).
Choir loft above a segmental arch decorated with spheres.
Recent discovery of an Isabelline coffered ceiling (1480), polychrome with vegetal motifs.

The Castle of the Counts of Orgaz, is from the 14th century, possibly built on a previous one, it is a beautiful example of a residential fortress and of magnificent conservation. It is rectangular in shape and finished with beautiful battlements with marvelous cantilevered sentry boxes on the corners. The Torre del Homenaje has a semicircular apse corresponding to a chapel that breaks the rectilinear structure of the complex.

The Town Hall is perhaps the most unique building in the municipality, representing a beautiful metaphor with its name and use.
Its architect was Ezequiel Martín Martín, and its construction began in 1927 and was completed practically a year later in 1928. The tile-clad tower was completed in 1930.
